observations.recommendation:
Given the foregoing inconsistencies in the presentation within the financial reports, we have recommended that the Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary corrections to the financial statements to accurately present the 2023 Government Equity, and to ensure that the restated beginning balance is correctly reflected in the 2024 financial statements, in compliance with the fair presentation requirement under Paragraph 27 of IPSAS; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to reassess the reconciliation process to ensure compliance with Paragraph 30 of IPSAS 2, and to verify the completeness and accuracy of the adjustments made from surplus to arrive at the net cash flows from operating activities;
Require the preparation of the necessary schedules and working papers and ensure their availability upon request, to facilitate proper verification and traceability of the amounts presented in the Statement of Cash Flows; and
Ensure that the inconsistencies identified in the comparative financial statements for 2024 are corrected to prevent the recurrence of such discrepancies in the preparation of future financial statements and ensure compliance with the fair presentation requirement under International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS).
The Management, in its response, assured the Audit Team that all inconsistencies in the report would be corrected to ensure compliance with the abovementioned standards.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entries to derecognize the asset accounts (Guaranty Deposits and Other Prepayments), as the related goods and services have already been received and utilized. The suggested adjusting journal entries are as follows:
Request the write-off of account balances for dormant receivable accounts totaling ₱160,000.00, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2023-008.
The Management has derecognized the asset accounts (Guaranty Deposits and Other Prepayments) through JEV No. 100-25-04-12.
Moreover, the Management commented that they will trace the beneficiaries and obtain certification from a reliable authority that the collectability of the account can no longer be reasonably ascertained, thereby providing a basis for the write-off of the dormant receivable account.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
Exhaust all possible means to establish contact with the creditor to facilitate the settlement of the outstanding obligation amounting to ₱89,296.88. However, in the event that all efforts have been exhausted and no formal communication or response is received from the creditor, the long-outstanding Accounts Payable should be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund, pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445, with the approval of the Sangguniang Bayan.
Submit the breakdown of Guaranty/Security Deposit Payable to identify which is from the bid documents, bail bonds, and performance bond. For bail bonds and performance bond, exhaust all possible means to refund the supplier/creditor.
The amount of ₱15,200.00, payable to three creditors, who have been confirmed deceased, may revert to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund, with approval of the Sangguniang Bayan, provided that all possible and lawful means to contact with the legal heirs for settlement of the claim have been exhausted, and the said amount remains unclaimed.
Management concurred with the recommendation and committed to settling the affected accounts and properly presenting them in the next reporting period.

We have recommended that the Management to:
Determine the variance between the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Appropriations (SCBAA) and the Utilization Report for the 20% Local Development Fund, and ensure that appropriate adjustments and corrections are made based on the source of the discrepancy;
Present the accurate amounts of the revenue items in the SCBAA that can be verified with the Statement of Financial Performance;
Properly classify the variance of P7,250.00 as MOOE under the LDRRMF in the SCBAA to accurately reflect the correct amount of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF transferred to STF in CY 2024; and
Present the accurate amount of consumed inventories as a reconciling item in order to properly reflect the correct amount of MOOE in the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Appropriations (SCBAA).
In response to the audit comments, the concerned offices are currently reconciling the entries to ensure that proper adjustments are reflected in the revised SCBAA, in accordance with IPSAS 24. Coordination between the Budget and Accounting Offices will be strengthened to ensure consistency in reported dates.
Management has taken steps to address the noted deficiencies and will continue to enhance internal controls related to the preparation and reconciliation of the SCBAA and related financial reports. Monthly reconciliations have been mandated to prevent the recurrence of similar issues. The corrected SCBAA, along with supporting schedules, will be submitted upon completion of the reconciliation.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to submit a list of unreleased checks to the Accounting Office at the end of each year. This is to prepare the Journal Entry Voucher, recognizing the restoration of the cash in bank equivalent to the unreleased checks, and to recognize the appropriate payable/liability accounts. This ensures the proper presentation of the aforementioned accounts in the year-end financial statements.
The Management affirmed the recommendation and assured the Audit Team that the affected accounts would be properly presented in the financial statements.

We have recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Treasurer to record all receipts and liquidations of cash advances, and to maintain separate cashbooks for each accountable officer handling Advances for Special Disbursing Officers under the General Fund (GF);
Except for cash advances for official travel, ensure that cash advances are granted only to officers or employees who are properly bonded and duly designated as Special Disbursing Officers.
The Management, in its response, assured the Audit Team that corrective action had been taken to update the cashbook to reflect the aforementioned advances and to ensure that all Special Disbursing Officers maintain accurate and up-to-date cashbooks.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation, giving emphasis on the importance of correcting the Work/Zoo Animals account by Management. To achieve this, necessary documents must be obtained. These documents include, among others, an Invoice Receipt of Property duly signed by an authorized official of the cooperative, an affidavit /certification from credible authorities as a basis, and a list of officials from the 5th District  Federation of Farmers Multi-Puroose Cooperative. Individual confirmation/demand letters should then be sent based on the list.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer/Property Officer to take appropriate action in maintaining Property Cards for all the PPEs of the LGU;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain  Property Ledger Cards and reconcile them accordingly with the Property Cards maintained by the designated Property Officer; and
Begin the process of complying with the One-Time Cleansing of PPE account balances to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the assertions regarding the existence, condition, and valuation of the PPE accounts.

We have recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to recognize the appropriate Semi-Expendable Expenses account for semi-expendable items purchased and issued during the calendar year 2024;
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entries to derecognize semi-expendable items purchased before CY 2024, which were originally recognized under the Property, Plant, and Equipment accounts. Ensure that the carrying amount of the items is expensed and charged to the Prior Period Adjustment, while the corresponding accumulated depreciation and accumulated impairment losses are closed in the books of accounts. The proposed adjusting entries are as follows:
To recognize adjustments due to change in accounting policy on capitalization threshold:
Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain the Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card (SPLC); and
Instruct the Inventory Committee and the Accountable Officer to prepare and maintain the forms, registries, and reports, except the SPLC, to enhance accountability and internal control over semi-expendable property.
Management concurred with the audit recommendation.

We reiterated our recommendation from last year that Management implement the following actions:
The Bids and Awards Committee should take immediate action to address the noted deficiencies and ensure that all documents identified in the auditorial review, as enumerated in Annex A, are submitted.
Instruct the Bids and Awards Committee, in collaboration with the Municipal Accountant, to ensure compliance with the documentary requirements outlined in COA Circular 2023-004. This will allow the Audit Team to fully assess projects and establish the validity, legitimacy, and propriety of the transaction.
The Management, in their response, submitted some of the previously unsubmitted documents identified in the issued AOM; however, certain documents remain outstanding, as shown in the table above.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation from last year that Management, through its Sangguniang Bayan, expeditiously process the review and approval of the revised Revenue Code, including the Market Code. This will yield new/additional revenue sources that will support the Municipality's programs/projects/activities, which will benefit the community's members.

We recommended to Management the following actions:
Strictly adhere to the documentary requirements enumerated under Section 5.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 when processing and granting applications for terminal leave benefits to officials and employees. We also recommended strict compliance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445 by ensuring that payments for claims with incomplete supporting documents are not authorized.
Submit the lacking documents to the Audit Team as soon as possible to avoid the issuance of a Notice of Suspension.
The Management has agreed to comply with the audit recommendation and submitted some of the previously unsubmitted documents identified in the issued AOM; however, certain documents remain outstanding, which we enjoin Management to submit.

We recommended that the Management:
Stop the conversion of leave credits unless the minimum requirement of 10 days is met and to ensure that at least 5 days are retained after monetization while strictly adhering to Section 22 of the Omnibus Rules on Leave;
Ensure rigorous oversight and regular updates of leave credits to avoid negative balances in the leave credit records kept by the Human Resource Management Office.
The Management commits to comply with the existing policies governing on this issue.

We have recommended that the Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and certify the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF using the required format. Submit it through the LDRRMO on or before the 15th day after the end of each month pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002, with copies furnished to OCD, DBM, and DILG.
Require the MDRRMO to submit the LDRRMFIP to the oversight bodies as required by Section 1.5.6 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
Avoid using the LDRRMF for expenditures that are prohibited under the provisions of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013, as this can lead to irregular expenditures and may result in a Notice of Disallowance.
Ensure adherence to current laws, rules, and regulations concerning the use of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund to strengthen the LGU's ability to enhance community disaster resilience, institutionalize measures for reducing disaster risks, and improve disaster preparedness and response capabilities at every level.
During the Exit Conference, the Management ensures that moving forward, the Utilization Report of LDRRMF will be prepared and certified by the Municipal Accountant.
Likewise, these reports will be submitted on or before the 15th day following the end of each month in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 with copies furnished to the OCD, DBM and DILG.
In addition, the Management in their response, have undertaken measures to ensure that all disbursements charged against the LDRRMF are in full compliance with the guidelines and are strictly limited to allowable activities and programs.

We have recommended Local Chief Executive of the following:
Require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to submit all financial transaction documents and related reports on time;
Implement a more streamlined process for managing the high volume of transactions, such as setting up a clear timeline for submitting reports with specific deadlines for each stage; and
Allocate additional personnel or resources to support the timely preparation and submission of financial reports.
Management indicated in its response that it will implement a more streamlined system to effectively manage the high volume of transactions and ensure the timely submission of all financial documents, transactions, and related reports to the Audit Team.

We have recommended that the Members of the Sangguniang Bayan immediately stop the practice of granting financial assistance/donations to their constituents as this is not among their mandated duties and functions under Section 447(a) of R.A. No. 7160 to avoid issuance of Notice of Disallowance in audit.
In its submitted reply, Management acknowledged that, while the grants were made in good faith and with the sincere intention of providing immediate support to their constituents, the disbursements fall outside the authorized functions of the Sangguniang Bayan. Management assured the Audit Team of its commitment to comply with the audit recommendation.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended the Management notify the Commission on Audit (COA) within twenty-four (24) hours following the acceptance of deliveries to ensure that inspections are conducted within the stipulated timeframe.
The Management commented that they will comply with the Audit recommendation.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that Management undertake the following measures:
Refrain from resorting to reimbursements through adequate procurement planning in accordance with Rule II Section 7 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184; and
Ensure direct payments to creditors or suppliers while properly withholding applicable taxes, in compliance to RMO No. 23-2014 dated June 20, 2014
Management concurs with the audit recommendation and has committed to its implementation.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management stop using the 20% Development Fund for expenditures that are prohibited under the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 dated November 4, 2020, as this can lead to irregular expenditures and may result in the issuance of a Notice of Disallowance.
The Management, in their submitted reply, committed to implementing corrective measures, including ceasing non-allowable charges to the 20% Development Fund, reclassifying disbursements, conducting capacity-building sessions, and ensuring compliance with auditing standards and fiscal management practices.

In light of the identified deficiencies, we recommended that Management, through the BAC, ensure the completeness and accuracy of the APP, including goods, infrastructure projects, services, and all necessary information, to maintain its continuous relevance and usefulness as a planning and control tool.
In response to the audit observations, Management, together with the BAC, commits to ensuring the completeness and accuracy of the APP, covering goods, infrastructure projects, services, and other necessary information, to maintain its relevance and effectiveness as a planning and control tool.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to deduct 10% retention money from the progress payment pursuant to Section 6.1, Annex E of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendation and will deduct 10% retention money from the progress payments moving forward.

We have recommended that Management:
Immediately stop the practice of issuing checks in the names of the Barangay Captain, Barangay Treasurer, SK Chairman, or SK Treasurer when providing financial assistance to the Barangay and the Sangguniang Kabataan. All financial assistance should be issued in the official name of the Barangay or SK as institutions;
Ensure that an Official Receipt (OR) issued by the Barangay or SK is attached to each disbursement voucher as part of the required supporting documents; and
Strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated 29 October 2012 to avoid issuance of Notice of Disallowance.
The Management has agreed to comply with the audit recommendation and will immediately cease the practice of issuing checks in the names of individual Barangay Captains/Treasurers or SK Chairpersons/Treasurers when providing financial assistance. Moving forward, all disbursements will be made payable to the Barangay or SK as the official recipient entity. Furthermore, an official receipt will be required and attached to each disbursement as a supporting document.

We have reiterated our recommendation from last year that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the BPLO, to devise measures to validate the declared gross receipts of establishments applying for permits to engage in business, especially those with direct transactions with the LGU;
Enforce the collection of underpayments, including the applicable surcharges and interest as provided in the Municipal Revenue Code. Non-compliance may necessitate the issuance of a corresponding Notice of Charge.
Include in the Instruction to Bidders or other documents issued by the Bids and Awards Committee that prospective suppliers/bidders should have no unpaid obligation with the Municipality as an eligibility requirement. A certification by the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant must be issued to effect this requirement.
The Management, in its response, contacted the suppliers involved and was able to promptly settle their outstanding tax liabilities.

We reiterated our prior year recommendation that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to collect business taxes from outside contractors who engage in projects within the Municipality, applying the situs of taxation. In this regard, issue demand letters to these contractors or require them to pay the accrued business taxes to the Municipal Government before being allowed to participate in the bidding process.
The Management has contacted the contractor involved and was able to settle the 2023 business tax liability. The contractor also committed to settling his business tax liability for 2024.

We recommended that the Management:
Ensure that at least five percent (5%) of the total budget is appropriated to activities that support Gender and Development (GAD) Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) in compliance with the provisions of Section 4, C.3.1 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 from PCW, DILG, DBM, and NEDA, in order to address more gender issues and concerns, and provide additional benefits to the constituents of the Municipality; and
Submit the Annual GAD Plan and Budget to the Provincial Planning and Development Office in accordance with Section 4, C.6.2 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 issued by PCW, DILG, DBM, and NEDA.
In its comment, Management expressed its commitment to comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team, providing reasonable assurance that the Agency will allocate 5% of its total budget to support GAD programs and activities, and to submit the Annual GAD Plan and Budget to PPDO.

We recommended that the Management:
Strictly stop utilizing the GAD Fund for expenditures that do not address gender related issues; and
Monitor the utilization and implementation of GAD PPAs to ensure that only GAD-related expenses are charged, and to identify and address any deviations or lapses in implementation.
The Management, in their response, assured that the GAD Fund will be allocated exclusively for activities that are directly aligned with the objectives of gender equality and women’s empowerment. Furthermore, it will be expended in strict accordance with the GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management:
Ensure that all stall owners have active contracts with the LGU.
Review the lease terms stipulated in the contract and ensure that reasonable rates are being charged.
Enforce collection of unpaid rent from delinquent stall owners.
Coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan for the updating of the Revenue Code and for the drafting a separate Market Code.
According to Management’s response dated May 20, 2025, the LGU, through the MPDO, is undertaking a comprehensive review of all lease contracts, with completion expected within the year. Updated contract templates will include defined lease periods and rental escalation clauses.
In addition, Management acknowledged that some lease terms reflect outdated rates due to past market conditions. To address this, Executive Order No. 13, s. 2023, created an Oversight Committee and Technical Working Group to rationalize lease structures and fees.
Furthermore, the Management confirmed that as of December 31, 2024, 14 stall owners were identified as delinquent, with total unpaid rent amounting to ₱573,989.25. Notices of Delinquency will be issued accordingly.
Management also agreed to draft a separate Market Code and will coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan to prioritize its passage. The MPDO and Engineering Office are finalizing the mapping and documentation of all government-leased properties, including updated cadastral maps and a GIS database.

We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entries to recognize the costs of the completed projects transferred to the LGU by the DPWH and the corresponding depreciations costs in the books of accounts. The suggested adjusting journal entries are as follows:

For the Bids and Awards Committee to take immediate action to address the noted deficiencies, and ensure that all lacking documents identified in the auditorial review, as enumerated in Annex A, are submitted.
Instruct the Bids and Awards Committee in coordination with the Municipal Accountant to ensure compliance with the documentary requirements as specified in COA Circular 2023-004 to enable the Audit Team to evaluate projects completely, establish the validity, legitimacy and propriety of the transaction.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

Review the errors noted in 2(e) and make necessary corrections in the cashbook.

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to strictly utilize the PCF for payment of authorized non-recurring petty operating or miscellaneous expenses which cannot be conveniently paid through checks; and

This will yield new/additional sources of revenues that will support the programs/projects/activities of the Municipality, advantageous for the members of the community.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to direct the Human Resource Officer, the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to comply with the requirements of the bank to ensure the immediate adoption of the ATM as a mode of disbursing salaries and other employees of the LGU on or before the end of May 2024.

We have recommended that Management stop the practice of purchasing funeral wreaths for its deceased constituents as it takes the nature of a personal expense and does not benefit the welfare of the general public.

We have recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to collect business taxes from outside contractors who engage in projects within the municipality applying the situs of taxation. We have recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the BPLO, to device measures to validate the declared gross receipts of establishments applying for permits to engage in business especially those who are having direct transactions with the LGU and its barangays.
Enforce the collection of the underpayment including the applicable surcharges and interests provided in the Municipal Revenue Code. Non-compliance may warrant the issuance of corresponding Notice of Charge.

We recommended that the Municipality ensure that at least five percent (5%) of the total budget be appropriated and shall correspond to activities supporting Gender and Development (GAD) Programs, Projects and Activities (PPAs) in compliance with the provisions of Section 4, C.3.1 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA in order to address more gender issues and concerns and provide more benefits to the constituents of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the local government unit strictly refrain from charging non-gender-responsive expenses against the fund allocated for Gender and Development initiatives. The Local Chief Executive should take proactive measures to ensure the proper implementation of the approved GAD Plan and Budget, in strict compliance with the provisions outlined in Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA in order to address more gender related issues and contribute to the realization of the country’s commitment to strengthening women’s empowerment, promoting gender equality, and fostering gender-inclusive development

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to the procedures in the establishment and replenishment of cash advance under Petty Cash Fund in accordance with Section 4.3.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 48 of Volume I of MNGAS for LGUs, and to limit the amount of cash advance to an amount sufficient for petty expenses of the Municipality for one month.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes at the beginning of the year for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant verify the balances of Deferred Real Property Tax and Deferred Special Education Tax with the balances of RPT and SET Receivables to determine the discrepancies and draw adjusting entries, if necessary.

The local government unit should exert diligent efforts to locate the necessary documents supporting their claims in order to establish the legitimacy of the receivable accounts.

When a receivable account is confirmed to be non-moving or remained to be inactive in the books of accounts for ten (10) years or more and where settlements or collectability could no longer be ascertained, it shall be considered a dormant receivable account. In this regard, management should be guided by procedures on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive –
Instruct the Municipal Assessor to provide this Office a photocopy of land titles to ascertain the legality of LGU’s ownership; and
Facilitate the processing of the TCT for all their untitled lots pursuant to Section 39 of PD 1445 and Section 449, Volume 1 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual to show conclusive proof of ownership of the LGU over these parcels of land.
We recommended further that the Appraisal Committee be guided by Department Order 37-09 dated October 19, 2009, Department of Finance, “Prescribing the Philippine Valuation Standard.”

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.
We further recommended the following courses of action:
Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network, and disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the total road network system;
Require the Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of General Services Officer, to maintain a Report of the Local Road Network and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components; and
Create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that in order that Work/Zoo Animals account balance can be corrected by Management necessary documents which will include among others an Invoice Receipt of Property duly signed by an authorized official of the cooperative and affidavit/certification from credible authorities to be used as basis.

We recommended that Management undertake the following actions in the disposal of unserviceable/obsolete property and equipment, to wit:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of General Services Officer, to report all unserviceable property and equipment of the LGU in the Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and ensure that the said report be duly accomplished;
Create a Disposal/Appraisal Committee to administer and manage the disposal process; and
Ensure that the unserviceable/obsolete property and equipment be disposed pursuant to Section 379 of RA No. 7160 and COA Circular No. 89-296 dated January 27, 1989.  Likewise, be guided accordingly with the guidelines set forth under Chapter 7 of the Training Handbook on Property & Supply Management System in the appraisal and disposal of properties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accoutant and Municipal Treasurer to transfer the remaining unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱1,974,510.47 under the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund in the Trust Fund books for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the required supporting documents are complete before affixing her signature on the voucher and effecting payment. This would also, in effect, avoid inconveniencies on the part of those who approved the payments such as suspension or disallowance of the transaction.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the BAC Chairman, BAC Secretary and Municipal Accountant to submit the required documents enumerated herein to establish the validity and propriety of the transaction.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the required supporting documents are complete before affixing her signature on the voucher and effecting payment. This would also, in effect, avoid inconveniencies on the part of those who approved the payments such as suspension or disallowance of the transaction.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to accept receipt in accordance with BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 2-2014 as evidence of payment in the procurement of goods.

We also recommended the submission of the Sales Invoice pertaining to the aforementioned transactions to support the payments made.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive –
Require the Acting Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents to support the payment made to the contractor;
Require the Contractor to correct the cracks and scaling of the concreted pavement, and impose liquidated damages due to incurrence of delay of 80 days; and
Require the Municipal Engineer to submit the Statement of Work Accomplished as of March 22, 2020.

We recommended to Management the following courses of action:
Limit the procurement of goods and services on a reimbursement basis, unless the circumstances warrant its application;
Require the Municipal Treasurer to adhere strictly to the provision that all payments should be made by check directly payable to the suppliers; and
Adopt public bidding or alternative modes of procurement provided under RA No. 9184 to promote transparency and to avoid possible source of irregularities.

We recommended to Management the adoption of the Online Payroll System by the LGU because it is a time and cost efficient scheme of disbursing payroll to its employees. Likewise, it prevents possible risk such as robbery, hold-up and similar incidents. Considering the benefits it could give to both management and employees, immediate implementation is hereby recommended.

We recommended that Management adopt the electronic or computerized system in collection and accounting which is a time and cost efficient scheme in processing transactions and in the preparation of reports. Likewise, it prevents errors and inconsistencies in processing and generating reports.
Considering the benefits it could give to both management and employees, we strongly recommended that computerization of government transaction be given priority, and a corresponding budget be appropriated for this purpose.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all claims against government funds are duly supported with complete documentation necessary in compliance with Section 4, paragraph 6 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445. All disbursement vouchers and supporting documents should also be properly accomplished, approved and certified.

To strengthen the internal control in the use of government vehicles and to regulate fuel consumption of the Municipality, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that at the end of every month, the agency submit to the COA Audit Team Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Reports of Official Fuel Consumption for audit purposes as required in COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977.

In addition, the following measures are hereby recommended:
For every use of government vehicle, ensure that it is supported by a duly approved and properly filled out Driver’s Trip Ticket; and
Ensure that all Driver’s Trip Ticket are properly numbered and recorded in a logbook for control purposes and maintain a separate file of the Driver’s Trip Ticket.

It is further recommended that policies and guidelines as clearly defined under COA Circular No. 77-61 be given due regard for proper control and monitoring of vehicle utilization and gasoline consumption.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations in the proper handling, custody and disposition of cash and recording of cash transactions to safeguard the government funds from risk of loss, theft or misuse.

For the foregoing deficiencies, we recommended the following:
The Municipal Treasurer require the collecting officers/field collectors to remit their collections intact on a daily basis and the Report of Collections and Deposits be prepared at the close of each business day; and
Checks to be released to the payees/claimants should be properly acknowledged by affixing their signatures in the Check Register.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the submission of the following reports and accounts within the prescribed period for timely review and verification thereof.

We also recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all accounts and reports for succeeding months be submitted on or before the 10th day of the following month to the Auditor pursuant to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06 to enable the conduct of timely audit of transactions and to ensure validity, completeness and accuracy of the recorded transactions.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that Management exert concerted effort to collect delinquent realty taxes by applying Section 254 of RA No. 7160.  For long outstanding delinquent accounts, Management may consider to impose civil remedies as provided under Section 174, Chapter IV of RA No. 7160 to enforce and intensify the collection of these accounts in order to generate additional income for the financial operation of the local government unit.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to exert more effort to collect the delinquent market stall rentals from stallholders by enhancing or improving the present collection strategy in order to generate more income for the efficient implementation of development programs/projects/activities beneficial to the constituents.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management –

Secure approval/authorization from local Sanggunian for the implementation of projects/programs/activities that were described in generic terms; and
Ensure that the 20% Development Fund is utilized within the purposes expressly prescribed by law in order to achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes and to avoid possible sanctions that may be imposed for non-compliance therewith.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with GAD Focal Person, to refrain from charging expenses that are not related in addressing gender issues and promoting women’s empowerment and gender equality as provided in the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, and to charge these expenses to other programs of the LGU where deemed appropriate.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point System to –

Institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation;
Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues; and
Revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.

We recommended to the Local School Board to include allocations from its Special Education Fund in support of the ECCD Program in compliance with RA No. 10410 and of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues; and

Revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the chairman of the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC), implement programs, projects and activities (PPAs) under LCPC in accordance with the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to address the needs of children and other related issues that need immediate attention and action. We have also recommended that members of the LCPC prepare Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children, and maximize the allocated LCPC Fund to identified PPAs for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries to fully achieve the objectives of the law creating the fund.

We reiterated our audit observation that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to the procedures in the establishment and replenishment of cash advance under Petty Cash Fund in accordance with Section 4.3.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 84, 85, and 86, Volume I of Government Accounting Manual for Local Government Units, and to limit the amount of cash advance to an amount sufficient for petty expenses of the Municipality for one month.

In response, Management expressed its willingness to comply with the given audit recommendations.

We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Local Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the current year based on Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes, and the corresponding income accounts at the beginning of the year.

Management commented that in order to comply with the audit recommendations and obtain accurate data, the management is considering the following actions:
Set aside fund for the pruning and cleansing of taxpayer’s database at the office of the Municipal Assessor so as to ensure the accuracy of the total collectible to be set up at the start of the year.
Hire additional personnel at the Assessor’s Office to focus in the cleansing of database since there are still properties that have duplicity and houses that are already demolished.
Management also informed the Audit Team that the setting up of RPT receivables for the year 2020 and 2021 was not merely based on estimates, but they were based on available data which is the 2013 Roll of Assessment provided by Office of the Municipal Assessor and diligently verified by the Office of the Municipal Treasurer one by one before causing collection to know if there are duplicities or deficiencies.
Management also assured the Audit Team that they are exerting all efforts to ensure that the audit recommendation on this matter will be fully implemented in the succeeding year.

In view of the foregoing deficiencies, we recommended the following –
The local government unit should exert diligent efforts to locate the necessary documents supporting their claims in order to establish the legitimacy of the receivable accounts;
When a receivable account is confirmed to be non-moving or remained to be inactive in the books of accounts for ten (10) years or more and where settlements or collectability could no longer be ascertained, it shall be considered a dormant receivable account. In this regard, management should be guided by procedures on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

Management commented that they are exerting efforts to locate the necessary documents for possible collection/settlement of accounts. As much as possible the LGU does not want to write-off the subject receivables because they are still looking forward for its possible settlement.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive the following:
To require the Municipal Accountant to record the purchase of construction materials through the Construction Materials Inventory account and maintain Supplies Ledger Cards.
For the Municipal Treasurer to maintain stock cards and submit the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) together with the original copy of the RIS to the Accounting Unit which shall be the basis in the preparation of Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to ensure that the receipt and issuances of construction materials are properly accounted and recorded as they take place in the proper asset accounts.

Management signified its willingness to comply with the recommended procedures by the Audit Team for the subsequent period.

We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Assessor to provide this Office a photocopy of land titles to ascertain the legality of LGU’s ownership. Also, facilitate the processing of the TCT for all untitled lots pursuant to Section 39 of PD 1445 and Section 449, Volume 1 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual to show conclusive proof of ownership of the LGU over these parcels of land.
We recommended further that the Appraisal Committee be guided by Department Order 37-09 dated October 19, 2009, Department of Finance, “Prescribing the Philippine Valuation Standard.”

Management commented that it has exerting its effort to secure conclusive proof of ownership of all parcel of land owned by the municipality. They are hoping that for the subsequent periods they could fully comply with the audit recommendations regarding this matter. However, there are some parcels of land that have low possibility of titling. Management also assured the Audit Team on the submission of status of subject lots.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that in order that Work/Zoo Animals account balance can be corrected by Management necessary documents which will include among others an Invoice Receipt of Property duly signed by an authorized official of the cooperative and affidavit/certification from credible authorities to be used as basis.

In response, Management commented that diligent efforts will be exerted to gather necessary documents in order to correct the said account balance. We were also informed that, as an initial action, the Management has reached out some credible authorities from the dissolved 5th District of Pangasinan Dairy Multi-Purpose Cooperative and they are responding favorably.

We recommended that Management stop from charging previous year’s expenditures to current appropriation pursuant to Section 119 of PD No. 1445. We also recommended that before the year end, the Municipal Accountant should set a deadline for the submission of all claims of officials and employees, and creditors in order to record the obligations of the agency and recognized the expenditures on the year they were actually incurred to avoid obligating the same in succeeding year’s appropriation.

Management commented that said transaction was actually charged to CY 2020 budget, however, missed to include it on the Accounts Payable account. We were also informed that Management is willing to make any adjustments and will take necessary measures for the subsequent period to avoid this kind of error.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units for accurate and reliable presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

Management informed the Audit Team that the Office of the Municipal Accountant is willing to make adjustments on the accounts affected. We were also assured that on the subsequent period the agency will strictly observe the proper accounts prescribed in the Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units.

We recommended that Management require the MSWD Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, to submit copy of Social Amelioration Card (SAC) Forms, copy of valid IDs, and pictures of beneficiaries during actual receipt of financial assistance for further evaluation of the Audit Team.
We further recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the descriptive title of accounts provided in the Chart of Accounts for LGUs to ensure correct accounts presented in the financial statements.

Management assured the Audit Team on the submission of the required documents.

We recommended that Management submit the abovementioned documents to the Audit Team to ensure the validity and propriety of the amount claimed. Strict compliance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 by not allowing payment for claims with incomplete supporting papers is further recommended.

Management assured the Audit Team on the submission of the required documents.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of granting Transportation Allowance (TA) to officials and employees with assigned government vehicle/s or are presently using government motor transportation pursuant to Section 8.2.1 of Local Budget Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2013.

Management commented that the abovementioned municipal vehicles which are apparently provided to certain officials who are also entitled to transportation allowance were actually used officially by any personnel under their direct supervision. With that, Management assumed that it was proper to issue Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) under their name.
However, they were informed through AOM that it is not in accordance with existing government rules and regulations. In response, Management submitted updated PAR of the subject vehicles under the name of those who have the actual custodianship.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations pertaining to granting and liquidation of cash advances. It is further recommended that:
The Municipal Administration stop the practice of granting cash advances to officials and employees who are not designated as disbursing officers and for purposes other than those allowed under Section 3 of COA Circular No. 97-002;
Require the Municipal Treasurer to make payment thru check directly in the name of the supplier or creditor instead of granting cash advances; and
Stop the granting of cash advances to elected officials other than for traveling expenses in compliance with Section 4.1.4 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

The Management of Local Government Unit of Laoac signified its willingness to comply with the audit recommendations and leaving assurance that for the ensuing year no more irregular cash advances will be granted.

To strengthen the internal control in the use of government vehicles and to regulate fuel consumption of the Municipality, we recommended that at the end of every month, the agency submit to the COA Audit Team Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Reports of Official Fuel Consumption for audit purposes as required in COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977.
In addition, the following measures are hereby recommended:
For motor vehicle which is not exclusively used by any government official, it is recommended that each time the vehicle is used, require that the vehicle be returned  in the office  full tank, thus the actual no. of liters consumed for each travel is immediately determined and the same shall be indicated in the Driver’s Trip Ticket; and
Ensure that all Driver’s Trip Ticket are properly numbered and recorded in a logbook for control purposes and maintain a separate file of the Driver’s Trip Ticket.
It is further recommended that policies and guidelines as clearly defined under COA Circular No. 77-61 be given due regard for proper control and monitoring of vehicle utilization and gasoline consumption.
The LGU Management agreed to comply with the recommended measures by the Audit Team for the subsequent period.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations in the proper handling, custody and disposition of cash and recording of cash transactions to safeguard the government funds from risk of loss, theft or misuse. For the foregoing deficiencies, we recommended the following:
The Municipal Treasurer require the collecting officers/field collectors to remit their collections intact on a daily basis and the Report of Collections and Deposits be prepared at the close of each business day;
Collections should not be utilized for encashment of private checks; and
Checks to be released to the payees/claimants should be properly acknowledged by affixing their signatures in the Check Register.

The Office of the Municipal Treasurer agreed to refrain from using collection for encashment of checks and to make strategies to comply with the given audit recommendations.

We recommended that Management reconsider to insure its buildings and other insurable assets to protect the interest of the municipal government and to be indemnified in case of damage or loss due to fortuitous events such as fire, earthquake, typhoon and/or flood.  In this regard, an updated inventory report of properties be submitted to the GSIS for appraisal purposes to determine the premium of insurance to be paid. Then, budget shall be allocated for this purpose.

Management commented that the non-insurance of the three (3) municipal buildings was due to insufficiency of funds. We were also informed that said buildings were not frequently used that is why they were not included in the priority list. Nonetheless, in order to fully comply with the given recommendations, Management will source out funds and insure those buildings with General Insurance Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive
Instruct the BAC Chairman, BAC Secretary and Municipal Accountant to submit the required documents enumerated herein to establish the validity and propriety of the transaction.
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required supporting documents are complete before affixing her signature on the voucher and effecting payment. This would also, in effect, avoid inconveniencies on the part of those who approved the payments.
Require the BAC and TWG to submit a written explanation why the estimated budget indicated in the submitted Supplemental APP was only ₱4,660,000.00 and why the Notice to Proceed was issued only to the successful bidder after twenty-two (22) calendar days from the date of approval of the contract contrary to Section 37.4.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

Management assured the Audit Team on the submission of the required documents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the BAC Chairman, BAC Secretary and Municipal Accountant to submit the required documents enumerated herein to establish the validity and propriety of the transaction.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the required supporting documents are complete before affixing her signature on the voucher and effecting payment. This would also, in effect, avoid inconveniencies on the part of those who approved the payments such as suspension or disallowance of the transaction.

Management assured the Audit Team on the submission of the required documents.

We recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report and approve by him and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and latest GPPB issuances.

The Bids and Awards Committee regrets the late submission of Procurement Monitoring Report, yet assured the Audit Team that on the subsequent period the PMR will be prepared and submitted in compliance with RA No. 9184 and GPPB issuances.

We recommended that Management release the share of the barangays from the basic real property tax earnings on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter in accordance with the provision of Section 271 of RA No. 7160 also known and cited as the Local Government Code of 1991 to enable the barangays to use the additional income for development projects which are beneficial to its constituents.

Management assured the Audit Team that on the subsequent period, the share of the barangays from the basic real property tax earnings will be released on time for better service delivery to their constituents.

We recommended that Management –
Require the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the submission of the following reports and accounts within the prescribed period for timely review and verification thereof.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all accounts and reports for succeeding months be submitted on or before the 10th day of the following month to the Auditor pursuant to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06 to enable the conduct of timely audit of transactions and to ensure validity, completeness and accuracy of the recorded transactions.

Management commented that as much as the Local Government Unit of Laoac desires to submit paid vouchers and payrolls, official receipts and its supporting documents within the reglamentary period, they find it very hard because of the manual processing of all the documents and insufficiency of funds to sustain the required manpower/technology resources to improve efficiency of financial services. Nonetheless, Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations by all means.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that Management exert concerted effort to collect delinquent realty taxes by applying Section 254 of RA No. 7160.  For long outstanding delinquent accounts, Management may consider to impose civil remedies as provided under Section 174, Chapter IV of RA No. 7160 to enforce and intensify the collection of these accounts in order to generate additional income for the financial operation of the local government unit.

Management commented that the Local Government Unit of Laoac could not possibly apply civil remedies against tax payers because their tax delinquency has not yet reached the threshold amount which is the assessed value of their subject properties. We were also informed that in order to intensify the collections, the following activities are taken to comply with the audit recommendations:
The Office of the Municipal Treasurer has been consistently sending Notices of Delinquency to taxpayers with delinquent accounts.
Work with the personnel of the Assessor’s Office to make sure that the taxpayers’ data are accurate.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to exert more effort to collect the delinquent market stall rentals from stallholders by enhancing or improving the present collection strategy in order to generate more income for the efficient implementation of development programs/projects/activities beneficial to the constituents.

Management commented that all delinquent stallholders are currently residing abroad. We were informed that Management had already sent series of notices but still not enough so they will use another scheme to collect from them or they may revoke or terminate their lease contract if the law permits so.

We recommended that Management –
Ensure that the projects/programs/activities designed under the 20% Development Fund are implemented fully within the time frame. Optimal utilization of the 20% IRA is also recommended in order to deliver the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to the constituents towards the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all; and
Secure approval/authorization from local Sanggunian for the implementation of projects/programs/activities that were described in generic terms.

Management regrets the low performance of their municipality having an annual completion rate of lower than 50% of its planned projects under the 20% Development Fund which is inconsistent with the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01. As in other areas of the country, Management explained that, as in other areas of the country, this maybe the common effect of the Covid-19 pandemic which restrained all socio-economic activities which started on March 15, 2020 due to the imposed lockdowns, community quarantines and the like to contain the spread of the dreadful virus.
Seemingly, this has been the reason why the Municipality of Laoac has been constrained to implement its various PPAs during the early quarters of the year. It was on the later part of the year when quarantines have been eased that priority development projects and related activities as embodied in the municipality’s AIP 2020 has been gradually implemented, hence the appropriated funds were only utilized during this period.
Management also atone to the identified five (5) projects under the 20% Development Fund costing ₱1,053,550.47 described in generic terms and were unseemly implemented without the required approval of the Sangguniang Bayan which is contrary to existing laws, rules and regulations.  Management assured that this error will be corrected in the succeeding years and will secure approval from the Sangguniang Bayan for the implementation of PPAs that are described in generic terms in their AIPs to adhere with Section 22 (c) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that Management stop from charging expenses not related in addressing gender issues and promoting women’s empowerment and gender equality as provided in the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01. We also recommended that the GAD Focal Person assist in determining GAD related issues in the locality which will be considered in the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality.

Management believes that allocations made on the GAD plan were for priority PPAs for Gender and Development and GAD Plan is in accordance with Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA. However, with the deficiencies noted in the post audit, Management expressed its willingness to improve the plan including its implementation to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point System to –
Institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation;
Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues; and
Revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.

Management is looking forward to the establishment of GAD database for the ensuing year. Management also expressed its intention to improve its GAD plan and analysis based on PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the Chairman of the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC), implement programs, projects and activities (PPAs) under LCPC in accordance with the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to address the needs of children and other related issues that need immediate attention and action. The members of the LCPC should maximize the allocated LCPC Fund to identified PPAs for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries to fully achieve the objectives of the law creating the fund.

Management believes that allocations made on the LCPC plan were for priority PPAs for the protection of children and have been identified according to their needs. However, with the deficiencies noted in the post audit, Management expressed its willingness to improve the plan including its implementation to comply with the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.

We recommended to the Local School Board to include allocations from its Special Education Fund in support of the ECCD Program in compliance with RA No. 10410 and of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

Management commented that the distribution of Special Education Fund to equitably support 19 elementary and 2 secondary public schools of the municipality is a big challenge for the local government.  Management explained that as much as the Local School Board wanted to allocate funds to support ECCD programs but because of the limited funds, allocations on ECCD programs were only programmed and mainstreamed under Gender and Development Fund.
However, Management explained that with the increasing income rate of the municipality, eventually, they will include allocations from the Special Education Fund to support ECCD programs in compliance with RA No. 10410 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that prior year’s unexpended balance of LDRRMF is included in the revised Annual Investment Program (AIP) duly approved by the local sanggunian and Local Chief Executive which shall serve as the basis in the utilization thereof.

Management commented that working with paranoia during the enhanced community quarantine period brought by the COVID-19 pandemic was a big challenge for everyone and the management’s priority to urgently deliver the required services to their constituents using the available funds has been one of the grounds to miss some technical procedures.
The Audit Team was informed that although the Management integrated the unexpended balances of LDDRMF accrued to Special Trust Fund amounting to Php3,010,040.22 in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Investment Plan, they regret that the same were not incorporated in the Revised Annual Investment Program.
Nonetheless, Management assured the Audit Team that the procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2012-002 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 will be strictly followed in the ensuing year.
We recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of taxes withheld.

We have recommended that the accounting and property records be updated and reconciled periodically to come up with a reliable PPE report and to present fairly the PPE accounts in the Statement of Financial Position. Other seven (7) audit recommendations were added to strengthen the internal control system of the LGU in property management, to wit:

Maintain/update the PPE ledger cards and Property Card.

Maintain/update the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR). It has to be renewed every three years or every time there is a change in custodianship/user of the property.

Affix property tags to each property and equipment during the physical inventory-taking for proper control and monitoring.

Make use of Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) in the issuance of semi-expendable items (such as punchers, staplers, scissors, etc,) for proper accountability and control. 

During  the physical inventory, ensure that all identified unserviceable property and equipment be reported in the Inventory and Inspection of Report of Unserviceable Property which is the basis of the Accountant to drop the unserviceable items from the respective PPE accounts and reclassify the same to Other Assets account pending its disposal. 

Submit Monthly Report on PPE Purchases with the following data to be prepared by a designated Property Officer.

Submit Monthly Report on Semi-Expendable Purchases with the following data to be prepared by a designated Supply Officer.
observations.managementAction:
Updated and maintains PAR.








Affixed tags on movable properties.
Reconciled accounts and CIP balances adjusted.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the accounting and supply records be updated and reconciled periodically to come up with a reliable and fair presentation of inventory accounts in the financial statements. 

Ensure that a physical inventory is regularly conducted on inventory items for each semester and the result of the physical count be reported in the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) which shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.

For other concerns on supply management and accounting, management should strictly adhere and be guided by Section 112 to 126 of Volume 1 of the LGU-Manual on the New Government Accounting System pending the implementation of Local Government Accounting Manual.
observations.managementAction: Conducted physical inventory and reconciled inventory accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting or correcting entries by recording the amounts payable to officers and employees to the Due to Officers and Employees account. Only amount of indebtedness arising from trade or business be recorded as Accounts Payable. 

Strict adherence of the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units is further recommended for accurate and reliable presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Affected accounts were adjusted.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Accounting Unit regularly review the transactions charged to Repairs and Maintenance expense accounts and effect an adjusting entry to record the capital expenditures in the books as asset in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 and IPSAS 1 for fair presentation of the Asset and Expense accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Affected accounts were adjusted.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant make necessary adjusting or correcting entries and strictly use the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units for accurate and reliable presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements. 

We have also recommended that the required documents be submitted to justify the payment for monetization of more than fifty percent (50%) of accumulated leave credits to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension.
observations.managementAction: Affected accounts were adjusted.
Funds liquidated.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management strictly adhere to Appendix 1, Guidelines on the Implementation of Infrastructure Projects by Administration of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 whenever it implements infrastructure projects by administration through Pakyaw System Contracting to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.
observations.managementAction: All infrastructure projects were implemented through straight contract except for those PPAs with ABC below Php100,000.00.

Establish internal control system to implement procedural guidelines on Shopping and Small-value procurement including the proper documentation for each procurement transaction. Partially implemented due to urgency of procurement caused by pandemic.

Due to pandemic which requires less travels and less exposure to hazards, the employees prefer one-time transaction.




The management does not grant cash advance to those employees with existing balances.




Religiously withhold from the employees’ salaries if cash advance is not settled within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that Management stop the granting of monthly cash incentive to former barangay officials and submit written explanation/s on why the amount of above-mentioned expenditures exceeded the amount of appropriation provided therefor under Municipal Ordinance No. 18-003 dated October 15, 2018 to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance. 

We have further recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units for accurate and reliable presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The management has stop granting monthly cash incentive to former barangay officials.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management strictly comply with the rates and schedule prescribed under COA Circular No. 2013-003A and the documentary requirements prescribed under Section 5.15 of COA Circular 2012-001 for the grant of Loyalty Award to qualified LGU officials or employees to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance.
observations.managementAction: The management has reviewed records of service of all employees and verified those granted loyalty awards and made appropriate actions to correct mistakes of previous year.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the Head of the Local School Board, adhere strictly to the existing laws, rules and regulations in the prioritization and utilization of Special Education Fund particularly for the operation and maintenance of public schools, construction and repair of school buildings, facilities and equipment, educational research, purchase of books and periodicals and sports development as provided in Sections 100(c) and 272 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 s. 2017 in order to ensure an effective educational development within the locality.
observations.managementAction: Prepared plan according to existing laws, rules and regulations in the prioritization and utilization of Special Education Fund.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we have recommended the following courses of actions –

Strict adherence to the documentary requirements enumerated under Section 5.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 in the processing and granting of applications for terminal leave benefits by its officials and employees; 

The Local Chief Executive require the Personnel Officer/HRMO and Municipal Accountant to submit the Daily Time Records (DTRs) for CY 2019 of all Public Health Workers and other concerned personnel to support the validity of the claims on hazard, subsistence and laundry allowances. Also, the Management should strictly observe the rules and regulations in granting hazard, subsistence and laundry allowances and ensure that all claims on said allowances be consistently supported with the required Daily Time Records and other required documents prior to payment pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012, IRR of RA 7305, and DOH-DBM Joint Circular No. 1, S. 2012.

Strict compliance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445 by not allowing payment for claims with incomplete supporting papers is further recommended.
observations.managementAction: In the ensuing year, management required those employees to submit their DTRs and other supporting documents for every claim on hazard pay, subsistence and laundry allowances.

observations.recommendation:
It has been recommended that the: 

Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry to effect the 15% and 5% share of the OBO and DPWH, respectively from the collection of building permit;

Municipal Treasurer strictly monitor the amount to be automatically retained with the Municipality to be used for OBO’s programs and projects and the amount to be remitted to BTr representing the 5% share of DPWH; and

Municipal Engineering Office as the OBO to prepare Program of Work detailing the manner on the utilization of the 15% share from the collection of building permits, and properly observe the guideline in the preparation of the program of works.
observations.managementAction:
Adjusted the accounts to effect the 5% and 15% sharing scheme.




Remitted to DPWH their 5% share.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the working area of the liquidating/disbursing/collecting officers of the Municipal Treasury Office, which also included the Business Permit Licensing Office, be properly enclosed and secured to safeguard money/cash collections, property and important documents from possible loss and theft.
observations.managementAction: Installed glass-type enclosure on the collection area at the Municipal Treasurer’s Office.

observations.recommendation: In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the concerned officials and employees submit the supporting documents being required for further evaluation of the COA ROI Technical Evaluation Services and Audit Team and in order to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension.
observations.managementAction: Returned unspent balance to DA, the sponsoring agency.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to transfer the remaining unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱1,662,844.50 under the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund in the Trust Fund books for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years.
observations.managementAction: Transferred said unexpended balance to Special Trust Fund Account.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management strictly observe the guidelines set forth in the aforementioned Circular and ensure that the unexpended balance of Trust Liabilities-DRRMF of previous years be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund only after the lapse of the five-year prescribed period which could then be used for other social services of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The reverted fund was only appropriated after the lapse of the five-year prescribed period. LDRRMF for the ensuing year is properly monitored.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the chairman of the MDRRMC, ensure compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations in the utilization of Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund in order to strengthen the capacity of the LGU to build the disaster resilience of communities, institutionalize measures for reducing disaster risks and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities at all levels.
observations.managementAction: Repair of CCTV was transferred/charged to other funds. Further, balance from previous funds was fully utilized to manage the effect of COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to maintain a separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations, and the Municipal Accountant to prepare and certify the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) and submit through the LDRRMO on or before the 15th day after the end of each month pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: Maintains separate registry/records for LDRRMF utilization.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the accounting and property records be updated and reconciled periodically to come up with a reliable PPE report and to present fairly the PPE accounts in the Statement of Financial Position. Other seven (7) audit recommendations were added to strengthen the internal control system of the LGU in property management, to wit:
Maintain/update the PPE ledger cards and Property Card.
Maintain/update the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR). It has to be renewed every three years or every time there is a change in custodianship/user of the property.
Affix property tags to each property and equipment during the physical inventory-taking for proper control and monitoring.
Make use of Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) in the issuance of semi-expendable items (such as punchers, staplers, scissors, etc,) for proper accountability and control.
