During  the physical inventory, ensure that all identified unserviceable property and equipment be reported in the Inventory and Inspection of Report of Unserviceable Property which is the basis of the Accountant to drop the unserviceable items from the respective PPE accounts and reclassify the same to Other Assets account pending its disposal.
Submit Monthly Report on PPE Purchases with the following data to be prepared by a designated Property Officer.
Submit Monthly Report on Semi-Expendable Purchases with the following data to be prepared by a designated Supply Officer.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Assessor to provide this Office a photocopy of land titles to ascertain the legality of LGU’s ownership. Also, facilitate the processing of the TCT for all their untitled lots pursuant to Section 39 of PD 1445 and Section 449, Volume 1 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual to show conclusive proof of ownership of the LGU over these parcels of land.
We have further recommended that the Appraisal Committee be guided by Department Order 37-09 dated October 19, 2009, Department of Finance, “Prescribing the Philippine Valuation Standard.”

We have recommended that in order that Work/Zoo Animals account balance can be corrected necessary documents which will include among others an affidavit/certification from credible authorities to be used as basis.

We have recommended that the accounting and supply records be updated and reconciled periodically to come up with a reliable and fair presentation of inventory accounts in the financial statements.
Ensure that a physical inventory is regularly conducted on inventory items for each semester and the result of the physical count be reported in the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) which shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
For other concerns on supply management and accounting, management should strictly adhere and be guided by Section 112 to 126 of Volume 1 of the LGU-Manual on the New Government Accounting System pending the implementation of Local Government Accounting Manual.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes at the beginning of the year for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
We have also recommended that the Municipal Accountant verify the balances of Deferred Real Property Tax and Deferred Special Education Tax with the balances of RPT and SET Receivables to determine the discrepancies and draw adjusting entries, if necessary.

The LGU should exert diligent efforts to locate the necessary documents supporting their claims in order to establish the legitimacy of the receivable accounts;
When a receivable account is confirmed to be non-moving or remained to be inactive in the books of accounts for ten (10) years or more and where settlements or collectability could no longer be ascertained, it shall be considered a dormant receivable account. In this regard, management should be guided by procedures on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts under COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting or correcting entries by recording the amounts payable to officers and employees to the Due to Officers and Employees account. Only amount of indebtedness arising from trade or business be recorded as Accounts Payable.
Strict adherence of the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units is further recommended for accurate and reliable presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to consider the following audit recommendations:
Exert diligent effort to locate the necessary supporting documents;
Ensure that unutilized fund transfer after the completion of projects or programs be fully liquidated according to the Memorandum of Agreement;
Reclassify accordingly all accounts which are not properly classified.
When accounts in the trial balance (except receivable accounts) are confirmed to be non-moving or remained to be inactive in the books of accounts for more than five (5) years, these shall be considered dormant per COA Circular No. 97-001 dated February 5, 1997. We have recommended that a write-off of accounts be requested to the Commission on Audit supported with the following documents:
List of available records and extent of validation made on the accounts; and
Certification and reasons why the books of accounts/records, financial statements/ schedules and supporting vouchers/documents cannot be located.

We have recommended that the Accounting Unit regularly review the transactions charged to Repairs and Maintenance expense accounts and effect an adjusting entry to record the capital expenditures in the books as asset in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 and IPSAS 1 for fair presentation of the Asset and Expense accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant make necessary adjusting or correcting entries and strictly use the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units for accurate and reliable presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements. We have also recommended that the required documents be submitted to justify the payment for monetization of more than fifty percent (50%) of accumulated leave credits to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension.

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the PCSO funds received to the General Fund and ensure that appropriations are provided therefor in the utilization thereof;
To submit copies of Memorandum of Agreement entered into between the LGU with PCSO for CY 2019 indicating the category of programs for which its allocation or funds may be utilized and the reportorial requirements pertaining to its utilization as stated in Section 40 of the 2016 Revised IRR for the Small Town Lottery Operations of the PCSO.

We have recommended that Management stop from charging previous year’s expenditures to current appropriation pursuant to Section 119 of PD 1445. We have also recommended that before the year end, the Municipal Accountant should set a deadline for the submission of all claims of officials and employees, and creditors in order to record the obligations of the agency and recognized the expenditures on the year they were actually incurred to avoid obligating the same in succeeding year’s appropriation. Non-compliance thereof shall result to the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We have recommended that Management strictly adhere to Appendix 1, Guidelines on the Implementation of Infrastructure Projects by Administration of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 whenever it implements infrastructure projects by administration through Pakyaw System Contracting to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We have recommended that Management stop the granting of monthly cash incentive to former barangay officials and submit written explanation/s on why the amount of above-mentioned expenditures exceeded the amount of appropriation provided therefor under Municipal Ordinance No. 18-003 dated October 15, 2018 to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.
We have further recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units for accurate and reliable presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Members of the Sangguniang Bayan  immediately stop the practice of granting financial assistance/donations to their constituents as this is not among their mandated duties and functions under Section 447(a) of R.A. No. 7160 to avoid the issuance of notice of disallowance. Appropriated funds for donations under the said office should be fully transferred to the MSWDO whose task is to implement such programs, projects and activities.

We have recommended that Management strictly comply with the rates and schedule prescribed under COA Circular No. 2013-003A and the documentary requirements prescribed under Section 5.15 of COA Circular 2012-001 for the grant of Loyalty Award to qualified LGU officials or employees to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance.

We have recommended that Management ensure that the projects/programs/activities designed under the 20% Development Fund are implemented fully within the time frame. Optimal utilization of the 20% IRA is also recommended in order to deliver the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to the constituents towards the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the Head of the Local School Board, adhere strictly to the existing laws, rules and regulations in the prioritization and utilization of Special Education Fund particularly for the operation and maintenance of public schools, construction and repair of school buildings, facilities and equipment, educational research, purchase of books and periodicals and sports development as provided in Sections 100(c) and 272 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 s. 2017 in order to ensure an effective educational development within the locality.

We have recommended to the Local School Board to include allocations from its Special Education Fund in support of the ECCD Program in compliance with RA No. 10410 and of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

To strengthen the internal control in the use of government vehicles and to regulate fuel consumption of the Municipality, we have recommended that at the end of every month, the agency submit to the COA Audit Team Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Reports of Official Fuel Consumption for audit purposes as required in COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977.
In addition, the following measures are hereby recommended:
For motor vehicle which is not exclusively used by any government official, it is recommended that each time the vehicle is used, require that the vehicle be returned  in the office  full tank, thus the actual no. of liters consumed for each travel is immediately determined and the same shall be indicated in the Driver’s Trip Ticket;
Ensure that all Driver’s Trip Ticket are properly numbered and recorded in a logbook for control purposes and maintain a separate file of the Driver’s Trip Ticket; and
Ensure that dates of travel per Driver’s Trip Ticket are consistent with the dates indicated in the Travel Order and Certificate of Appearance.
It is further recommended that policies and guidelines as clearly defined under COA Circular 77-61 be given due regard for proper control and monitoring of vehicle utilization and gasoline consumption.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended the following courses of actions –
Strict adherence to the documentary requirements enumerated under Section 5.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 in the processing and granting of applications for terminal leave benefits by its officials and employees;
The Local Chief Executive require the Personnel Officer/HRMO and Municipal Accountant to submit the Daily Time Records (DTRs) for CY 2019 of all Public Health Workers and other concerned personnel to support the validity of the claims on hazard, subsistence and laundry allowances. Also, the Management should strictly observe the rules and regulations in granting hazard, subsistence and laundry allowances and ensure that all claims on said allowances be consistently supported with the required Daily Time Records and other required documents prior to payment pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012, IRR of RA 7305, and DOH-DBM Joint Circular No. 1, S. 2012.
Strict compliance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445 by not allowing payment for claims with incomplete supporting papers is further recommended.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations on the proper handling, custody and disposition of cash and recording of cash transactions to safeguard the government funds from risk of loss, theft or misuse. For the foregoing deficiencies, we have recommended the following:
The Municipal Treasurer require the collecting officers/field collectors to remit their collections intact or on a daily basis and the Report of Collections and Deposits be prepared at the close of each business day;
Collections should not be utilized for encashment of private checks; and
Checks to be released to the payees/claimants should be properly acknowledged by affixing their signatures in the Check Register.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to the procedures in the establishment and replenishment of cash advance under Petty Cash Fund in accordance with Section 4.3.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 48, Vol. I of MNGAS for LGUs, and to limit the amount of cash advance to an amount sufficient for petty expenses of the Municipality for one month.

We have recommended that Management reconsider to insure its buildings and other insurable assets to protect the interest of government and to be indemnified in case of damage or loss due to fortuitous events such as fire, earthquake, typhoon and/or flood.  In this regard, an updated inventory report of properties be submitted to the GSIS for appraisal purposes to determine the premium of insurance to be paid. Then, budget shall be allocated for this purpose.

We have recommended that the working area of the liquidating/disbursing/collecting officers of the Municipal Treasury Office, which also included the Business Permit Licensing Office, be properly enclosed and secured to safeguard money/cash collections, property and important documents from possible loss and theft.

We have recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report and approve by him, and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We have recommended that management release the share of the barangays from the basic real property tax earnings on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter in accordance with the provision of Section 271 of Republic Act 7160 also known and cited as the Local Government Code of 1991 to enable the barangays to use the additional income for development projects which are beneficial to its constituents.

We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the submission of the following reports and accounts within the prescribed period for timely review and verification thereof.
We have also recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all accounts and reports for succeeding months be submitted on or before the 10th day of the following month to the Auditor pursuant to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06 to enable the conduct of timely audit of transactions and to ensure validity, completeness and accuracy of the recorded transactions.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the concerned officials and employees submit the supporting documents being required for further evaluation of the COA ROI Technical Evaluation Services and Audit Team and in order to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension.

We have recommended that Management exert concerted effort to collect delinquent realty taxes by applying Section 254 of RA 7160.  For long outstanding delinquent accounts, Management may consider to impose civil remedies as provided under Section 174, Chapter IV of R.A. 7160 to enforce and intensify the collection of these accounts in order to generate additional income for the financial operation of the LGU.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to exert more effort to collect the delinquent market stall rentals from stallholders by enhancing or improving the present collection strategy in order to generate more income for the efficient implementation of development programs/projects/activities beneficial to the constituents.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of taxes withheld.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the GSIS regulations remittance of monthly personal share of employees and the contribution for employer’s share.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of Pag-IBIG premiums.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of PhilHealth premiums.

We have recommended that Management stop from charging expenses not related in addressing gender issues and promoting women’s empowerment and gender equality as provided in the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01. We have also recommended that the GAD Focal Person assist in determining GAD related issues in the locality which will be considered in the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point System to –
Institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation;
Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues; and
Revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the chairman of the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC), implement programs, projects and activities (PPAs) under LCPC in accordance with the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to address the needs of children and other related issues that need immediate attention and action. We have also recommended that members of the LCPC prepare Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children, and maximize the allocated LCPC Fund to identified PPAs for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries to fully achieve the objectives of the law creating the fund.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accoutant and Municipal Treasurer to transfer the remaining unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱1,662,844.50 under the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund in the Trust Fund books for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years.

We have recommended that Management strictly observe the guidelines set forth in the aforementioned Circular and ensure that the unexpended balance of Trust Liabilities-DRRMF of previous years be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund only after the lapse of the five-year prescribed period which could then be used for other social services of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the chairman of the MDRRMC, ensure compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations in the utilization of Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund in order to strengthen the capacity of the LGU to build the disaster resilience of communities, institutionalize measures for reducing disaster risks and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities at all levels.

We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to maintain a separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations, and the Municipal Accountant to prepare and certify the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) and submit through the LDRRMO on or before the 15th day after the end of each month pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We have recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be strictly complied. Thus, require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require the Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of General Services Officer, to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. Moreover, create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Physical Inventory conducted; Currently updating the Road Map

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant course the purchase of construction materials thru the Construction Materials Inventory account and maintain Supplies Ledger. We have further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer also maintain stock cards and submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) together with the original copy of the RIS to Accounting Unit which shall be the basis in the preparation of Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) in compliance with the provisions of Sections 114, 120 and 121 of Chapter 7, Volume I of the Manual New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) to ensure that the receipt and issuances of construction materials are properly accounted and recorded as they take place in the proper asset accounts.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Internal control for inventories is already present.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the local government unit seek legal assistance from Provincial Legal Office or Prosecutor’s Office in compliance with the provisions of Section 481 of RA 7160 and Revised Guidelines on the Deputation of Private Lawyers and Special Attorneys dated December 15, 2015.  We have further recommended that the LGU stop the practice of using government funds to pay the services of private lawyer to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Requested legal counsel from Solicitor General. The Legal Counsel of the LGU has resigned.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management submit a legal basis to justify the hiring of consultant who resigned from the government service but still within the prohibition period of one year from retirement as set forth in Section 7 of RA 6713, otherwise, all payments related thereto shall be refunded. In the absence of legal basis or justification, we recommend that Management stop from hiring an official or employee separated from public service until the prohibition period of one year from retirement/resignation had ended pursuant to the above-mentioned provision of the Code of Conduct and Ethical Standards for Public Officials and Employees.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Consultancy contract was not renewed.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management submit supporting documents to justify the adoption of Negotiated Procurement thru emergency purchase instead of competitive bidding. The Bids and Awards Committee, who has the responsibility for ensuring that the procuring entity abides by the standards set forth by RA 9184 and its IRR, should strictly adhere with all provisions of law, rules and regulations pertaining to procurements to protect at all times the interest of the local government unit.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Submitted documents to the Office of the Auditor.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that Management submit the required documents to justify the recommendation of the Bids and Awards Committee to resort to negotiated procurement and to provide a more holistic evaluation/review of all contracts.

Strict compliance with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and documentary requirements provided under COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 for negotiated procurement in case of two-failed biddings is further recommended.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | To strictly adhere to RA 9184 with regard to Negotiated Procurement.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the local government unit adhere with the existing guidelines on the manual labor component of projects undertaken by administration and submit supporting documents showing compliance thereof.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Complied with the given recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the BAC should post the procurement opportunity in the PhilGEPS website for a period of at least three (3) calendar days to comply with Sections 54.2 and 54.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184. Also, the Municipal Accountant ensure that the printout copy of the posting in the PhilGEPS website be included as one of the supporting documents for the payment of goods or services procured under shopping or small value procurement.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Starting July 2019, the BAC ensures posting of procurement opportunity whether under shopping or small value procurement mode in the PhilGEPS website. Printout copy of the said posting is also attached in the disbursement voucher.

observations.recommendation:
We have reiterated our previous year audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to comply with Section 7 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA 9184 and Updated Budget Operations Manual for Local Government Units in the preparation of Project Procurement Management Plan to be consolidated into an Annual Procurement Plan.  The BAC shall carefully plan and schedule the agency’s procurement in order to manage its resources, produce economical result, and make the procurement procedures more efficient and practical to avoid splitting of contracts which is not allowed by law. 

Ensure that the prescribed format under DBM Circular Letter 2011-6 be strictly followed in the preparation of Annual Procurement Plan for Common-Use Supplies and Equipment  to come up with a realistic budget in the procurement of common use supplies and equipment.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | To prepare PPMP.

observations.recommendation: In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the Head of the Agency require the BAC or Acting Supply/Property Officer to submit within the prescribed period of five (5) working days copies of perfected contracts, approved POs, and supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001 for a systematic and effective review process to come up with timely, relevant audit results. Administrative disciplinary action as provided in Section 4 of the same circular against the concerned employee be imposed upon unjustified failure of compliance.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Requires more manpower/time.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that Bank Reconciliation Statements for CY 2018 be submitted within fifteen (15) days after the end of each month pursuant to Section 74 of P.D. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-011 so that errors/omissions, if any, will be detected immediately and be corrected and adjusted accordingly in the books of accounts, thus, ensuring accuracy and reliability of the Cash in Bank account in the financial statements. 

When the bank could not furnish a bank statement on time, we have recommended that management advise the Municipal Accountant to enroll in an online banking services where bank statement information could be obtain any time to facilitate the timely preparation of the BRS, or obtain bank snapshots on the next banking day at the end of each month. Official Bank Statements and negotiated checks will still be submitted to COA Office when received from the bank.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Requires more manpower/time.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate 1 % of the IRA for the implementation of programs on child delinquency in compliance with the provision of Section 15 of Chapter I, Title III of R.A. 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120. We have also recommended that formulation of programs and activities funded therefrom is geared towards child protection and ensure proper implementation of programs in order to prevent juvenile delinquency in the locality.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Submitted Budget and Utilization Report on LCPC Fund for CY 2019.
AAR 2018 | Prepared ADA.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management strictly observe the guidelines set forth in the aforementioned Circular and revert back the unexpended balance of Trust Liabilities –DRRM for CY 2012 from Trust Fund to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund which could be used for other social services of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | To retransfer and include it in the LDRRMFIP and Supplemental AIP.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive should instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that prior year’s unexpended balance of LDRRMF be included in the revised Annual Investment Plan (AIP) duly approved by the Local Sanggunian and Local Chief Executive which shall serve as the basis in the utilization thereof.

We have also recommended that the MDRRMC prepare and formulate the disaster risk and reduction management programs/projects/activities of the previous year’s balances of LDRRMF which shall be incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.  The LDRRMFIP shall also include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities recommended to be charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | To include it in the LDRRMFIP and Supplemental AIP.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the accounting and property records be updated and reconciled periodically to come up with a reliable PPE report and to present fairly the PPE accounts in the Statement of Financial Position. Other seven (7) audit recommendations were added to strengthen the internal control system of the LGU in property management, to wit:
Maintain/update the PPE ledger cards and Property Card.
Maintain/update the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR). It has to be renewed every three years or every time there is a change in custodianship/user of the property.
Affix property tags to each property and equipment during the physical inventory-taking for proper control and monitoring.
Make use of Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) in the issuance of semi-expendable items (such as punchers, staplers, scissors, etc,) for proper accountability and control.
During  the physical inventory, ensure that all identified unserviceable property and equipment be reported in the Inventory and Inspection of Report of Unserviceable Property which is the basis of the Accountant to drop the unserviceable items from the respective PPE accounts and reclassify the same to Other Assets account pending its disposal.
Submit Monthly Report on PPE Purchases  with the following data to be prepared by a designated Property Officer.
Submit Monthly Report on Semi-Expendable Purchases  with the following data to be prepared by a designated Supply Officer.

We have recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be strictly complied. Thus, require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require the Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of General Services Officer, to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. Moreover, create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive to facilitate the processing of the TCT for all their untitled lots pursuant to Section 39 of PD 1445 and Section 449, Volume 1 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual to show conclusive proof of ownership of the LGU over the lots.
We have also recommended that an Appraisal Committee be created to determine the market value of the real property in the absence of historical cost which will be composed of the Municipal Assessor, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Treasurer.
We have recommended further that the Appraisal Committee be guided by Department Order 37-09 dated October 19, 2009, Department of Finance, “Prescribing the Philippine Valuation Standard.”

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant RPTAR or taxpayer’s index cards which shall be the basis in setting-up the RPT/SET Receivables in the books in order to reflect the actual collectibles from real property tax in the financial statements of the Municipality.

We have recommended that in order to establish the legitimacy of the Receivable accounts, the LGU should take efforts to locate the necessary documents supporting their claims, demand settlement of unliquidated cash advances and payment of liquidated damages from contractors. LGU should also strictly observe the policies as regards granting of loans to cooperatives. It should also consider aging of receivables and the option of request for write-off guided by procedures on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts under COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the noted variance/difference between the recorded book balance of Loans Payable - Domestic against the amount confirmed by LBP-Urdaneta to reflect the correct balance, to locate the necessary supporting documents, liquidation of unutilized fund transfer after the completion of projects or programs, and the proper classification of accounts.
Also, write-off of accounts could be considered for the inactive accounts guided by procedures on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts under COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We have recommended that the accounting and supply records be updated and reconciled periodically to come up with a reliable and fair presentation of inventory accounts in the financial statements.
Ensure that a physical inventory be conducted on inventory items for each semester and the result of the physical count be reported in the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) which shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
For other concerns on supply management and accounting thereof, those concern should be guided by Section 112 to 126 of Volume 1 of the LGU-Manual on the New Government Accounting System pending the implementation of Local Government Accounting Manual.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the proper accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units for accurate and reliable presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant course the purchase of construction materials thru the Construction Materials Inventory account and maintain Supplies Ledger. We have further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer also maintain stock cards and submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) together with the original copy of the RIS to Accounting Unit which shall be the basis in the preparation of Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) in compliance with the provisions of Sections 114, 120 and 121 of Chapter 7, Volume I of the Manual New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) to ensure that the receipt and issuances of construction materials are properly accounted and recorded as they take place in the proper asset accounts.

We have recommended that the Members of the Sangguniang Bayan  immediately stop the practice of granting financial assistance/donations to their constituents as this is not among their mandated duties and functions under Section 447(a) of R.A. No. 7160 to avoid the issuance of notice of disallowance in audit. Appropriated funds for donations under the said office should be fully transferred to the MSWDO whose task is to implement such programs, projects and activities.

We have recommended that the local government unit seek legal assistance from Provincial Legal Office or Prosecutor’s Office in compliance with the provisions of Section 481 of RA 7160 and Revised Guidelines on the Deputation of Private Lawyers and Special Attorneys dated December 15, 2015.  We have further recommended that the LGU stop the practice of using government funds to pay the services of private lawyer to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that Management submit a legal basis to justify the hiring of consultant who resigned from the government service but still within the prohibition period of one year from retirement as set forth in Section 7 of RA 6713, otherwise, all payments related thereto shall be refunded. In the absence of legal basis or justification, we recommend that Management stop from hiring an official or employee separated from public service until the prohibition period of one year from retirement/resignation had ended pursuant to the above-mentioned provision of the Code of Conduct and Ethical Standards for Public Officials and Employees.

We have recommended that Management submit supporting documents to justify the adoption of Negotiated Procurement thru emergency purchase instead of competitive bidding. The Bids and Awards Committee, who has the responsibility for ensuring that the procuring entity abides by the standards set forth by RA 9184 and its IRR, should strictly adhere with all provisions of law, rules and regulations pertaining to procurements to protect at all times the interest of the local government unit.

We have recommended that the Municipal Administration stop from granting cash advances to officials and employees for purposes other than those allowed under Section 3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
We have further recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to make payment thru check directly in the name of the supplier or creditor instead of granting cash advances.  Strict compliance with the provisions of the above-cited circular is further recommended to avoid issuance of notice of disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that the management ensure that the projects/programs/activities designed under the 20% Development Fund are implemented fully within the time frame. Optimal utilization of the 20% IRA is also recommended in order to deliver the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to the constituents towards the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the Head of the Local School Board, adhere strictly with the existing laws, rules and regulations in the prioritization and utilization of Special Education Fund particularly for the operation and maintenance of public schools, construction and repair of school buildings, facilities and equipment, educational research, purchase of books and periodicals and sports development as provided in Sections 100(c) and 272 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 s. 2017 in order to ensure an effective educational development within the locality.

We have recommended to the Local School Board to include allocations from its SEF in support of the ECCD Program in compliance with RA No. 10410 and of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that Management insure its buildings and other insurable assets to protect the interest of government and to be indemnified in case of damage or loss due to   fortuitous events such as fire, earthquake, typhoon and/or flood.  In this regard, an updated inventory report of properties be submitted to the GSIS for appraisal purposes to determine the premium of insurance to be paid. Then, budget shall be allocated for this purpose.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations in the proper handling, custody and disposition of cash and recording of cash transactions to safeguard the government funds from risk of loss, theft or misuse. For the foregoing deficiencies, we have recommended the following:
Collections should be remitted to the Municipal Treasurer promptly or on a daily basis and the same should be deposited with the authorized government depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day;
Cashbooks should be updated and reconciled with the records of the Municipal Accountant to ascertain the accuracy and reliability of the balances; and
Checks to be released to the payees/claimants should be properly acknowledged by affixing their signatures in the Check Register.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to the procedures in the establishment and replenishment of cash advance under Petty Cash Fund in accordance with Section 4.3.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 48, Vol. I of MNGAS, and to limit the amount of cash advance to an amount sufficient for petty expenses of the Municipality for one month.

We have recommended that Management submit the required documents to justify the recommendation of the Bids and Awards Committee to resort to negotiated procurement and to provide a more holistic evaluation/review of all contracts.
Strict compliance with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and documentary requirements provided under COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 for negotiated procurement in case of two-failed biddings is further recommended.

We have recommended that the local government unit adhere with the existing guidelines on the manual labor component of projects undertaken by administration and submit supporting documents showing compliance thereof.

We have recommended that the BAC should post the procurement opportunity in the PhilGEPS website for a period of at least three (3) calendar days to comply with Sections 54.2 and 54.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184. Also, the Municipal Accountant ensure that the printout copy of the posting in the PhilGEPS website be included as one of the supporting documents for the payment of goods or services procured under shopping or small value procurement.

We have reiterated our previous year audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to comply with Section 7 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA 9184 and Updated Budget Operations Manual for Local Government Units in the preparation of Project Procurement Management Plan to be consolidated into an Annual Procurement Plan.  The BAC shall carefully plan and schedule the agency’s procurement in order to manage its resources, produce economical result, and make the procurement procedures more efficient and practical to avoid splitting of contracts which is not allowed by law.
Ensure that the prescribed format under DBM Circular Letter 2011-6 be strictly followed in the preparation of Annual Procurement Plan for Common-Use Supplies and Equipment  to come up with a realistic budget in the procurement of common use supplies and equipment.

To strengthen the internal control in the use of government vehicles and to regulate fuel consumption of the Municipality, we have recommended that at the end of every month, the agency submit to the COA Audit Team Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Reports of Official Fuel Consumption for audit purposes as required in COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977.
In addition, the following measures are hereby have recommended:
For motor vehicle which is not exclusively use by any government official, it is have recommended that each time the vehicle is used, require that the vehicle be returned  in the office  full tank, thus the actual no. of liters consumed for each travel is immediately determined and the same shall be indicated in the Driver’s Trip Ticket;
Ensure that all Driver’s Trip Ticket are properly numbered and recorded in a logbook for control purposes and maintain a separate file of the Driver’s Trip Ticket; and
Ensure that dates of travel per Driver’s Trip Ticket are consistent with the dates indicated in the Travel Order and Certificate of Appearance.
It is further recommended that policies and guidelines as clearly defined under COA Circular 77-61 be given due regard for proper control and monitoring of vehicle utilization and gasoline consumption.

We have recommended that the Head of the Agency require the BAC or Acting Supply/Property Officer to submit within the prescribed period of five (5) working days copies of perfected contracts, approved POs, and supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001 for a systematic and effective review process to come up with timely, relevant audit results. Administrative disciplinary action as provided in Section 4 of the same circular against the concerned employee be imposed upon unjustified failure of compliance.

We have recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the submission of the following reports and accounts within the prescribed period for timely review and verification thereof.
We have also recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all accounts and reports for succeeding months be submitted on or before the 10th day of the following month to the Auditor pursuant to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06 to enable the conduct of timely audit of transactions and to ensure validity, completeness and accuracy of the recorded transactions.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that Bank Reconciliation Statements for CY 2018 be submitted within fifteen (15) days after the end of each month pursuant to Section 74 of P.D. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-011 so that errors/omissions, if any, will be detected immediately and be corrected and adjusted accordingly in the books of accounts, thus, ensuring accuracy and reliability of the Cash in Bank account in the financial statements.
When the bank could not furnish a bank statement on time, we have recommended that management advise the Municipal Accountant to enroll in an online banking services where bank statement information could be obtain any time to facilitate the timely preparation of the BRS, or obtain bank snapshots on the next banking day at the end of each month. Official Bank Statements and negotiated checks will still be submitted to COA Office when received from the bank.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to submit monthly trial balance on the 20th day of the following month and ensure that the required supporting schedules to the trial balance are also submitted in compliance with the above-cited provisions.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the concerned LGU officials and employees exert extra effort to intensify collection of delinquent accounts and adopt the procedures and civil remedies provided under Section 254, 256 and 174 of Republic Act No. 7160, otherwise known as the Local Government Code of 1991, to enable the collections of unpaid local taxes. These will provide additional funds to support the projected expenditure of the LGU which may be beneficial to its constituents.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of taxes withheld.

We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the GSIS regulations remittance of monthly personal share of employees and the contribution for employer’s share.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of Pag-IBIG premiums.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of PhilHealth premiums.

We have recommended that Management stop from charging expenses not related in addressing gender issues, promote women’s empowerment, and gender equality as provided in the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-01; and to charge it to other programs of the LGU.  We have also recommended that the GAD Focal Person assist in determining GAD related issues in the locality which will be considered in the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate 1 % of the IRA for the implementation of programs on child delinquency in compliance with the provision of Section 15 of Chapter I, Title III of R.A. 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120. We have also recommended that formulation of programs and activities funded therefrom is geared towards child protection and ensure proper implementation of programs in order to prevent juvenile delinquency in the locality.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accoutant and Municipal Treasurer to transfer the remaining unexpended balance of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱924,841.30 under the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund in the Trust Fund books for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years.

We have recommended that Management strictly observe the guidelines set forth in the aforementioned Circular and revert back the unexpended balance of Trust Liabilities –DRRM for CY 2012 from Trust Fund to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund which could be used for other social services of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive should instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that prior year’s unexpended balance of LDRRMF be included in the revised Annual Investment Plan (AIP) duly approved by the Local Sanggunian and Local Chief Executive which shall serve as the basis in the utilization thereof.
We have also recommended that the MDRRMC prepare and formulate the disaster risk and reduction management programs/projects/activities of the previous year’s balances of LDRRMF which shall be incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.  The LDRRMFIP shall also include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities recommended to be charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years.

We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to maintain a separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations, and the Municipal Accountant to prepare and certify the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) and submit through the LDRRMO on or before the 15th day after the end of each month pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Accounting Division verify the journal entries made relative to the recognition of completed projects in the books of accounts and make the necessary correcting entries for fair presentation of the PPE accounts in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
To strengthen the internal control system of the LGU in property management and to ascertain the accuracy, validity and existence of the property, plant and equipment as presented in the financial statements,  we hereby reiterated our previous years audit recommendations which were not fully implemented, to wit:
Create an  Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of  properties of the municipality. Be reminded that  actual count could be undertaken any month during the current  year, but it has to be updated at year-end. A copy of the Inventory Report has to be submitted to COA not later than January 31of each year.
Reconcile the PPE per inventory report with accounting records/reports. Any discrepancies shall be verified and adjusted accordingly.  Recipient of any missing PPEs be held accountable.
Maintain/update the PPE ledger cards and Property Card.
Maintain/update the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR). It has to be renewed every three years or every time there is a change in custodianship/user of the property.
Affix property tags to each property and equipment during the physical inventory-taking for proper control and monitoring.
Make use of Invenory Custodian Slip (ICS) in the issuance of semi-expendable items (such as punchers, staplers, scissors, etc.) for proper accountability and control.
During  the physical inventory, ensure that all identified unserviceable property and equipment be reported in the Inventory and Inspection of Report of Unserviceable Property which is the basis of the Accountant to drop the unserviceable items from the respective PPE accounts and reclassify the same to Other Assets account pending its disposal.
Submit Monthly Report on PPE Purchases  with the following data to be prepared by a designated Property Officer.
Submit Monthly Report on Semi-Expendable Purchases  with the following data to be prepared by a designated Supply Officer.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Assessor to provide this Office a photocopy of land titles for  parcels of land to ascertain the legality of LGUs ownership over eight (8) parcels of land. Further,  exhaust all possible means to document the legality of ownership of 33 lots through a Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) in the name of the Municipal Government of Laoac pursuant to Section 39 of PD 1445 and Section 449, Volume 1 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual.  Ensure that a budget shall be allocated for this purpose.
We also recommended that an Appraisal Committee be created to determine the market value of the real property in the absence of historical cost. The composition of the committee be patterned after Section 1 of the Miscellaneous Provisions (Chapter VIII) of the Manual on Real Property Appraisal & Assessment Operations dated January 2006, to wit:
Chairperson	:	Municipal Assessor
Member	:Municipal Engineer
Member	:Municipal Treasurer
We recommended further that the Appraisal Committee be guided by Department Order 37-09 dated October 19, 2009, Department of Finance, “Prescribing the Philippine Valuation Standard.”

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to consider the following audit recommendations:
Maintain individual subsidiary ledger for all accounts, as required under Sections 111 and 114 PD No. 1445 to record the vital information or details of the transactions.
Exert diligent effort to locate the necessary supporting documents. Prepare an   ageing schedule of liability accounts that would show the age of payables/liability per transaction.
Ensure that unutilized fund transfer after the completion of projects or programs be fully liquidated according to Memorandum of Agreement.
Reclassify accordingly all accounts which are not properly classified.
When accounts in the trial balance (except receivable accounts) are confirmed to be non-moving or remained to be inactive in the books of accounts for more than five (5) years, these  are considered to be dormant per COA Circular No. 97-001 dated February 5, 1997. We recommend that a write-off of accounts be requested to the Commission on Audit supported with the following documents:
List of available records and extent of validation made on the accounts; and
Certification and reasons why the books of accounts/records, financial statements/ schedules and supporting vouchers/documents cannot be located.

We recommended that management of the LGU initiate to gather evidence that the ownership of said imported cows was not transferred to the Municipality of Laoac. Such evidence, in the absence of Invoice Receipt of Property, would be sufficient to derecognize  Work/Zoo animals account amounting to  P17,670,747.14 in the books of account, thus the LGU be relieved from such accountability and Work/Zoo animals account be presented fairly in the financial statement.

To address the foregoing issue, remedies set forth under Section 174 of RA 7160, is hereby reiterated as follows:
The civil remedies for the collection of local taxes, fees, or charges, and related surcharges and interest resulting from delinquency shall be:
(a)	By administrative action through distraint of goods, chattels, or effects, and other personal property of whatever character, including stocks and other securities, debts, credits, bank accounts, and interest in and rights to personal property, and by levy upon real property and interest in or rights to real property; and
(b)     By judicial action.
Either of these remedies or all may be pursued concurrently or simultaneously at the discretion of the local government unit concerned.
The non-collection of arrearages on local taxes deprived the municipality of additional income which could have been used to pay other mandatory obligations or to implement other priority development projects/programs which are beneficial to its constituents.
We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the concerned LGU officials and employees exert extra effort to intensify collection of delinquent accounts and adopt the procedures and remedies provided in Section 254 and 256 of Republic Act No. 7160, otherwise known as the “Local Government Code of 1991, to enable the collections of unpaid local taxes.  These will provide additional funds to support the projected expenditure of the LGU which may be beneficial to its constituents.
We also recommended that records of the Municipal Accounting and Treasury Office be reconciled accordingly.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following actions to address the above issues and concerns, to wit:
The Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the Municipal Assessor prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in the setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes at the beginning of the year for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
The Municipal Accountant has to credit the receivable account upon collection while the deferred account is debited upon recognition of income and due to LGUs for the share of province and barangays.
Ensure that penalties and discounts are accurately computed and properly recorded in the books of accounts, and  properly apportioned to concerned LGUs in accordance with the sharing prescribed under the Local Government Code.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to implement the following audit recommendations, to wit:
Exert diligent effort to locate the necessary supporting documents. Prepare an   ageing schedule of receivable accounts that would show the age of receivables per transaction.
When these receivable accounts are confirmed to be non-moving or remained to be inactive in the books of accounts for ten (10) years or more and where settlements or collectability could no longer be ascertained, it shall be considered a dormant receivable account. In this regard, be guided by procedures on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts, unliquidated cash   advances, and fund transfers under COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
Ensure that no additional loans/ cash advances be granted unless the previous   loans/cash advances have been duly settled/ liquidated.
Reclassify cash advances granted to the principals from Due from Officers and Employees account to Due from NGAs account because the principals were not officers or employees of the Municipality but employees of DepEd which is a national government agency.
Ensure that the amount of additional loan to be granted to cooperatives shall  be based on its capacity to pay by reviewing its financial statements and by conducting an ocular inspection on its operation.

We recommended that the Accounting Division verify the journal entries made relative to the recognition of completed projects in the books of accounts and make the necessary correcting entries  for fair presentation of the PPE accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the accounting and supply records be updated and reconciled periodically to come up with a reliable and fair presentation of inventory accounts in the financial statements.
Ensure that a physical inventory be conducted on inventory items for each semester and the result of the physical count be reported in the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) which shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
For other concerns on supply management and accounting thereof, please be guided by Section 112 to 126 of Volume 1 of the LGU-Manual on the New Government Accounting System pending the implementation of Local Government Accounting Manual.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive submit legal basis in granting communication allowance to the Senior Legal Consultant. In the absence of legal basis, cause the immediate refund of such allowances.
For succeeding transactions of similar nature, we recommended that Management be guided with the above-cited CSC rules and regulations to avoid audit disallowance  if no legal basis could be presented to justify the claim.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure compliance with DBM Compensation Policy Guidelines No. 98-1 dated March 23, 1998, and refrain from granting Honorarium to BIR Collection Agent to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit.  In addition, we recommended that Management submit legal basis in giving honoraria to the BIR Collection Agent, otherwise, cause the refund of the honorarium.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterate our previous year audit recommendation that  encourage the adoption of the  Online Payroll System  by the LGU because it is a time and cost efficient scheme of disbursing payroll to its employees. Likewise, it prevents possible risk such as robbery, hold-up and similar incidents. Considering the benefits it could give to both management and employees, immediate implementation is hereby recommended.

In view of the foregoing,  we  reiterated our previous year audit recommendation that  encourage the adoption of  electronic or computerized system in assessment, collection and accounting which is a time and cost efficient scheme in processing transactions and in the preparation of reports. Likewise, it prevents errors and inconsistencies in processing and generating reports. Considering the benefits it could give to both management and employees, we recommended that computerization of government transaction be given importance, thus, a corresponding budget be appropriated for this purpose.

We reiterated our previous year audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to comply with Section 7 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA 9184 and Updated Budget Operations Manual for Local Government Units in the preparation of Project Procurement Management Plan to be consolidated into an Annual Procurement Plan.  The BAC shall carefully plan and schedule the agency’s procurement in order to manage its resources, produce economical result, and make the procurement procedures more efficient and practical to avoid splitting of contracts which is not allowed by law.
Ensure that the prescribed format under DBM Circular Letter 2011-6 be strictly followed in the preparation of Annual Procurement Plan for Common-Use Supplies and Equipment  to come up with a realistic budget in the procurement of common use supplies and equipment.

We reiterate our previous year audit recommendation that the Bids and Awards Committee prepare and submit to the Head of the Procuring Entity the Procurement and Monitoring Report and submit the same to the GPPB pursuant to IRR of RA 9184.
We further recommended that the BAC furnish us copy of the said report for verification and validation purposes on the status of the procurement process. It is also a useful tool for review of contracts related to the procurement of goods and infrastructure projects of the Municipality.

We recommended that submission of trial balance on the 20th day of the following month and submission of interim reports on the 20th day after the end of each quarter  be observed by the Accounting Office  pursuant to with Section 70 and 73, Volume 1 of the MNGAS. Ensure that the necessary attachment to the trial balance be also submitted in compliance with the above provision.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately all unsubmitted Bank Reconciliation Statements to the COA Office. Ensure that BRS for CY 2018 be submitted within fifteen (15) days after the end of each month pursuant to Section 74 of P.D. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-011 so that errors/omissions, if any, will be detected immediately and be corrected and adjusted accordingly in the books of accounts, thus, ensuring accuracy and reliability of the Cash in Bank account in the financial statements.
When the bank could not furnish a bank statement on time, we recommended that management advise the Municipal Accountant to enroll in an online banking services where bank statement information could be obtain any time to facilitate the timely preparation of the BRS, or obtain bank snapshots on the next banking day at the end of each month. Official Bank Statements and negotiated checks will still be submitted to COA Office when received from the bank.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterate our audit recommendation that the Head of the Agency require the BAC/ Acting Supply/Property Officer to submit within the prescribed period of five (5) working days copies of approved POs, perfected contracts and supporting documents to COA auditor as required under COA Circular No. 2009-01 for a systematic and effective review process to come up with timely, relevant audit results.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require all department heads of the LGU to purchase common-use supplies and equipment at the Procurement Service pursuant to Administrative Order No. 17 s.  2011 and DBM Circular Letter No, 2011-6 to avail the most advantageous price for the government. When goods are to be purchased from local/private suppliers, ensure that these shall always be supported by an evidence of non-availability of stocks in the PS and shall be purchased at reasonable prices.

We recommended that Management reconsider to insure its buildings and other insurable assets to protect the interest of government and to be indemnified in case of  damaged or loss due to   fortuitous events such as  fire, earthquake, typhoon and/or flood.  In this regard, an updated inventory report of properties be submitted to the GSIS for appraisal purposes to determine the premium of insurance to be paid. Then, budget shall be allocated for this purpose.
We recommended that management continue to implement BIR rules and regulations to help the government generate more revenues for the benefit of the general public.

We recommended that management continue to deduct premiums from the salaries and wages of LGU- Laoac employees and to provide the corresponding  government share, and remit the same with the GSIS pursuant to RA No. 8291 for the social security protections of government workers and employees.
